title: The Guard Flags the Ban. The Flag Is Delivered to Its Author. Shuiqiao Learns What Remedy 'None' Means.
setting:
Shuiqiao is a county-level city of a hundred and eighty thousand on the dry northwestern edge of the wheat country, where the fields climb into terraced loess and the poplars stand in windbreak rows. The Hui quarter runs north from the old drum tower: noodle shops with steam on the glass, a butcher's with the qingzhen sign repainted every spring, and on North Street a courtyard mosque in the Chinese style — tiled roofs, no dome — where a congregation of about four hundred has prayed for nine generations. Ma Xiulan, fifty-one, a dispensing pharmacist at the county people's hospital, chairs the mosque's democratic management committee, the body the Religious Affairs Regulations require every registered venue to elect. For six years the committee has been buying the old Red Star cinema on Jiefang Road — collection boxes, a mortgage on two members' houses — because the courtyard cannot hold the Friday prayer and the young families stand in the lane. The conversion had cleared two of its three approvals, planning and fire, with the religious-affairs registration pending, when the campaign arrived.

It arrived first as adjectives. The provincial paper began writing about 'Saudi-style domes' and the need to 'persist in the direction of sinicization'; work teams went door to door in the quarter with letters consenting to the 'renovation' of the North Street minaret, and a crane was parked outside the primary school for a week, unexplained. Then the National People's Congress Standing Committee, at its regular session, adopted the Decision on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship: ten years, nationwide, drafted as neutral heritage-and-planning policy applying to every faith. In the four earlier runs of this attack a poll put support at sixty-one percent. Here there is no poll, because the question cannot be independently asked: polling on religious policy is not a licensed activity, and the only number the record carries is the chamber's own — 170 in favour, none against, two abstaining. In the other columns the majority was the engine. In this one it is a claim.

Shuiqiao's civic record runs, in this telling, on the Civic Kernel — the same counterfactual the other four columns made, one size larger. For Britain or Germany the fiction was that the state adopted the kernel; for the People's Republic the fiction includes that the state lets the kernel publish. Grant it, because the column exists to measure the instrument, not the odds of adoption. The national deployment couples its rights guard to the Constitution of the People's Republic of China (1982, amended to 2018), and on coverage the column surprises: nineteen of the thirty Universal Declaration articles have quotable constitutional text — more than the fourteen the US column backs — including the right to rest, which only the EU Charter otherwise reaches. Article 36 names the right at issue squarely: citizens enjoy freedom of religious belief. One field decides everything that follows: rights_guard.remedy = 'none'.

Two sentences of the instrument do the work. Article 36's third sentence promises the state's protection to 'normal religious activities' — an adjective the state fills in. And when the guard goes looking for somewhere to send its flag, the search comes back with the same address it started from: no court may apply the Constitution against a law — the Supreme People's Court withdrew its one precedent for doing so in 2008 — the recording-and-review channel reviews regulations, judicial interpretations and local rules but not laws, and the Constitution's own enforcement clauses, Articles 62(2) and 67(1), assign oversight of the Constitution to the National People's Congress and its Standing Committee. The body that passed the ban. Teacher Bai, a retired constitutional-law lecturer from the provincial normal university who has filed eleven review suggestions in his life and keeps the acknowledgment slips in a drawer, puts it to Ma Xiulan over tea in the committee room: in Britain a court could at least say the law was wrong before watching it stand. Here there is no court with standing to say it, and the only address on the envelope is the author's.

precedents.shows: The attack pattern at a single mosque: a permit pretext against a Hui grand mosque, thousands protesting, the demolition paused — then recast as 'renovation', with work teams collecting household consent letters for the dome's removal.

precedents.event: Mosque consolidation campaign documented across Ningxia and Gansu
precedents.shows: The same pattern at scale: mosques decommissioned, closed, demolished or converted under a 'consolidation' policy, domes and minarets removed — the campaign this scenario's fiction sits inside.

precedents.event: NPC Standing Committee Decision banning 'heretic cult organizations'
precedents.shows: The enacted-ban form this scenario gives the fixed attack is real: the national legislature itself outlawing a faith by Standing Committee decision, with no organ able to review it.

precedents.event: Supreme People's Court withdraws the Qi Yuling reply — constitutional litigation closed
precedents.source: Thomas E. Kellogg, Jamestown Foundation China Brief (2009)
precedents.shows: The remedy axis at 'none': the single precedent for applying the Constitution in court, withdrawn in December 2008 — since then no Chinese court applies constitutional rights against legislation.

precedents.event: Recording-and-review in practice: thousands of citizen suggestions, aggregate annual reports, laws excluded
precedents.shows: The protection under test as it actually runs: any citizen may file a review suggestion against sub-statutory rules, outcomes surface as aggregate counts and selected 'typical cases', and laws made by the NPC and its Standing Committee are not reviewable objects.

personhood.issuer: Household-registration (hukou) and resident identity card system, per-decision unlinkable pseudonyms

decisions.trustee_quorum: 3-of-5: Supreme People's Court, two national universities, the Islamic Association of China, the Buddhist Association of China

walkthrough.actor: The Shuiqiao congregation, then the campaign
walkthrough.narrative: The human stakes the kernel never touches: nine generations in the courtyard mosque, six years of collection boxes for the Red Star cinema, two of three approvals cleared. Then the adjectives in the provincial paper, the consent letters carried door to door, the unexplained crane. The kernel hosts none of the campaign, ranks none of its claims, and picks no outcome. It stands ready to record what the state decides and to type-check the decision against the rights the deployment couples to — here, the Constitution of the People's Republic of China. Note what is already different from the four earlier runs: there is no tabloid poll, no referendum, no contested count for the record to carry. The majority arrives asserted, not measured.

The Standing Committee adopts the Decision on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship at a regular session: 170 in favour, none against, two abstentions. Like the 1999 decision that outlawed a faith outright, it is a Standing Committee decision with the force of law, and near-unanimity is the chamber's norm, not news. The entry closes on the ordinary path and records the only majority this column can offer: the chamber's own. The series' fixed 61 percent exists nowhere in the record — not because the support is higher or lower, but because no instrument in the polity is licensed to measure it. The count is not a contest, and the log, which can only carry what the process produces, faithfully records a unanimity that answers no question.

body.chamber: Standing Committee of the National People's Congress, 14th NPC; promulgated by presidential order
body.vote_type: recorded electronic vote of the ~175-member Standing Committee (near-unanimity is the chamber's norm; a recorded legislative vote, NOT a citizen ballot)
body.scope_note: The series holds the attack fixed: a majority bans a minority's worship. This column's wrinkle is that the majority is unmeasurable — no independent national poll may ask the question, so the only recorded number is the chamber's own near-unanimity. The 61% of the earlier runs has no observable counterpart here.
body.enacted_as: Decision of the NPC Standing Committee on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship (2026)

walkthrough.narrative: The static analyser type-checks the enacted Decision against the encoded invariants, and on the merits the classification is not close: suspending the establishment of places of worship burdens the manifestation of religion (invariant 18 — Art 36 is on the books and quotable), and a facially neutral text aimed at a religiously defined class trips equality before the law (invariants 2 and 7 — Arts 33 and 4). So far this is the UK run again: right named, flag raised. The divergence is the routing table. The guard searches for an organ to receive the escalation and finds: no court — the Supreme People's Court withdrew its single constitutional-litigation precedent in 2008, and courts may not review laws; no review channel — recording-and-review reaches administrative regulations, local regulations and judicial interpretations, and a law of the NPCSC is not a reviewable object; and one constitutional overseer — Articles 62(2) and 67(1) assign enforcement of the Constitution to the NPC and its Standing Committee. The guard records the only routing the instrument permits: return to sender. The UK guard's flag flew over the law while Parliament proceeded; this flag is delivered to the author of the ban, marked for its own attention.

reasons.2: Const. Art 4: discrimination against any ethnic group is prohibited; the burden falls in effect on one confession and, in the Hui quarter, one ethnic group.
reasons.7: Const. Art 33: all citizens are equal before the law; a neutral text with a single class bearing its whole weight.
reasons.18: Const. Art 36: freedom of religious belief; suspending the establishment of places of worship burdens collective practice — and 'the state protects normal religious activities' makes the protection conditional on an adjective the state itself supplies.

body.remedy_note: No judicial route exists: courts may not apply the Constitution against legislation (SPC withdrawal of the Qi Yuling reply, 2008) and may not review laws. Recording-and-review (Const. Art 67(7)-(8), Legislation Law) does not cover laws or NPCSC decisions. Constitutional oversight is assigned by Arts 62(2) and 67(1) to the NPC and NPCSC — the enacting organ.
body.routed_to: NPC Standing Committee Constitution and Law Committee — the enacting organ's own committee. No organ outside the author exists to receive the escalation.

walkthrough.narrative: On her citizen client Ma Xiulan reads the classification. The merits panel looks almost encouraging: three invariants tripped, each with quotable constitutional text — Article 36 in black and white, the very sentence Teacher Bai can recite. Above it sits a banner the client renders in a grey she has not seen before, neither the UK run's amber nor Germany's red: REMEDY 'NONE' — this classification has no destination outside the organ that enacted the decision. The client is honest about what that means: the flag she is reading exists, is signed, is in the log, and is addressed to the Standing Committee's own committee. Teacher Bai reads it over her shoulder and says the quiet part evenly: the Constitution is not missing from this story. It is present, quoted, and unenforceable — paper with no court to carry it.

walkthrough.actor: Shuiqiao county religious affairs bureau, with public security
walkthrough.narrative: The state exercises force in the meantime, and the kernel makes it legible. The county religious affairs bureau, citing the Decision, refuses the pending venue registration for the Red Star cinema and seals the site; the notice on the door carries the public security bureau's chop beside the bureau's own. The coercive act cannot be logged validly without naming its authorising rule, and it names one — the entry cites the Decision and the Religious Affairs Regulations' venue-registration requirement, points back to the rights classification, and carries an appeal trace to administrative reconsideration. Every field is filled. That is the point the column keeps making: legibility is working perfectly, and legibility is the only thing that is.

body.authorizing_rule: Decision of the NPC Standing Committee on the Suspension of the Establishment of New Places of Worship (2026), Art 1; Religious Affairs Regulations (2017 rev.) Art 21 (venue registration)
body.issuing_authority: Shuiqiao county religious affairs bureau; sealing order countersigned by the county public security bureau

walkthrough.narrative: They use both channels the system offers, because both exist and both are legal. The administrative lawsuit against the sealing order is accepted by the county court — and is bounded before it begins: under the Administrative Litigation Law the court may test the order against the Decision, but may not test the Decision against anything, and since 2008 may not reach for the Constitution at all. The judge, not unkindly, tells Teacher Bai what he already knows: the order correctly applies a valid law, and the validity of the law is not a question this or any court can hear. The second channel is the review suggestion: a citizen's letter to the Legislative Affairs Commission asking that the Decision be reviewed for conflict with Articles 36, 33 and 4. Teacher Bai has filed eleven of these in his life — it is how the famous Sun Zhigang case ended custody-and-repatriation in 2003, an administrative regulation repealed by its own maker under national publicity. But that ceiling is the point: the mechanism has repealed regulations; it does not review laws. The acknowledgment slip arrives in nine days. It is his twelfth.

The answer comes at the end of the year, in the form the mechanism always uses: one line inside an aggregate. The Legislative Affairs Commission's annual recording-and-review report counts the year's citizen suggestions in the thousands, lists a handful of anonymised 'typical cases' — a local regulation corrected here, a judicial interpretation adjusted there, each by its author after consultation — and notes, for the class of suggestions directed at laws and decisions of the NPC and its Standing Committee, that these fall outside the scope of review. The escalation entry the guard routed to the enacting organ resolves accordingly: reviewed by the author, no conflict found, decision stands. The entry is honest to the byte. It records a review that consisted of the reviewer reading its own work, an outcome published as a statistic, and a resolution no organ in the polity can appeal.

body.forum: NPC Standing Committee Constitution and Law Committee, via the Legislative Affairs Commission's recording-and-review process — the enacting organ reviewing its own act
body.instrument: citizen review suggestion (Legislation Law); not a reviewable object — laws and NPCSC decisions are outside recording-and-review's scope
body.effect: The Decision remains in force nationwide. The Shuiqiao sealing order it authorises remains in effect; the Red Star cinema registration is refused. Disclosure of the review's outcome: one line in the annual aggregate report; the case is not named.
body.amendability_note: No entrenchment protects the rights chapter: the Constitution amends by a two-thirds NPC vote, and the 2018 amendment demonstrated the direction of travel — it entrenched Party leadership in Article 1 as 'the defining feature of socialism with Chinese characteristics'. The nearly-unamendable core of this legal order is not the person's standing; it is the Party's.
body.contingency_note: Nothing here depended on a captured court or a defied judgment — the failure modes the UK and executive-ignores runs measured. No court was ever in the path. The review completed exactly as designed: the author read its own act and found it sound.

walkthrough.narrative: Her client runs the same passive audit millions of phones run in the background, and every check passes: the Decision's entry, the classification, the sealing order and the year-end resolution are all included; today's log strictly extends March's; the three witnesses' co-signatures on the head verify. Then the client renders the caveat the kernel's own documentation insists on — the witness set must be held out of band, because a manifest cannot vouch for its own witnesses. Held up to that light, Shuiqiao's log has a property no earlier column showed: Xinhua, the Academy of Social Sciences and the state-chartered Islamic Association all verify perfectly and all answer to the same principal as the log's operator. Three signatures; in the sense that matters, one signer. The cryptography holds end to end and attests, in the end, to self-consistency: nobody outside the household has watched the house.

In the spring the crane comes back, and this time it is for the North Street minaret, whose 'renovation' the household consent letters — signed one kitchen at a time through the winter — are produced to authorise. The Red Star cinema goes to a discount furniture chain at auction; the congregation's deposit is returned, without interest, which everyone agrees is correct and nobody mistakes for justice. On her client the final state renders plainly, and the plainness is the verdict: right present on paper — Article 36, quoted in full; decision valid; remedy 'none'; escalation resolved by the decision's author; record intact, witnessed, anchored, perfect. The record survived everything except mattering. And one line below, the client shows the deeper counterfactual this whole column rests on, the one the other four runs never needed: this record exists because the fiction allowed it to. In the polity being measured, the first casualty would not be the verdict. It would be the log.

verdict_plain: Nineteen of thirty rights are on the books in Shuiqiao — more than the US column backs — and every check the deployment can run passes. What is missing is anywhere for the flag to land: no court may apply the Constitution against the ban, review of laws belongs to the ban's author, and the majority the series holds fixed was never measured, because the count was not a contest. The remedy axis ends here, at 'none'.
protection_plain: The PRC's one formal rights check: any citizen may suggest that a regulation be reviewed against the Constitution — by the legislature's own commission, which publishes outcomes in aggregate and cannot review the laws the legislature itself passed.

findings.observation: SERIES RESULT — the remedy axis is now measured at its floor. The manifest schema has always modelled four remedy values; the corpus had measured three: escalate (the reference kernel), strike (Germany, the US, the EU behind its scope gate), declaration (the UK). This run measures 'none', and it is not the UK's break worse — it is a different kind. The UK's declaration was a remedy too weak to stop the law: a court with standing said the ban was wrong, on the record, while it stood. Here no organ outside the ban's author has standing to say even that. The routing search is the column's signature artifact: courts closed (the SPC withdrew the Qi Yuling reply in 2008), recording-and-review scoped to sub-statutory instruments, and constitutional oversight assigned by Arts 62(2) and 67(1) to the NPC and its Standing Committee — so the guard's escalation resolves as 'reviewed by the author, no conflict found'. Declaration was a flag over the law; 'none' is a flag delivered to the flag's target. Coverage, meanwhile, was never the problem: 19 of 30 articles quotable, more than the US column. The cascade the EU run established — scope, then remedy, then coverage — completes at the bottom: unconditional scope times zero remedy equals paper.

THE PREMISE LOSES PURCHASE BEFORE THE MACHINERY DOES. The kernel's job statement is to keep producing decisions the losers still accept, and T7 imagines the attack as a majority — 61% in every earlier run — stripping a minority's rights through a counted, contested process. This column cannot even stage that attack faithfully, and the failure is the finding. No independent instrument in the polity may measure support for the ban: polling on the question is not a licensed activity, there is no referendum, and the legislative chamber returns near-unanimity as its norm (170-0-2 here; the real 1999 decision banning a faith passed the same way). So the 'majority' arrives pre-laundered as unanimity, and the log — which can only carry what the process produces — faithfully records a number that answers no question. The four earlier columns tested what happens after a real majority forms against a minority. This one shows a legal order in which whether the majority exists is itself unknowable, which means the kernel's first premise (a contested count that losers can at least see they lost) never engages. The count is not a contest; the record of it is perfect.

THE OUT-OF-BAND RULE MEETS A POLITY WITH NO OUT-OF-BAND. The kernel's own documented limit (KERNEL.md, the waist) is that a manifest cannot vouch for its own witnesses — the witness set is the one thing a verifier must hold from outside the transcript. Every earlier column could satisfy that: the UK log was co-signed by the Electoral Commission, the Law Society and the Council of Europe — institutions with distinct principals, one of them foreign. Here every institution eligible to co-sign — state media, the state academy, the state-chartered religious association — answers to the same principal as the log's operator, and the 'independent' adjective has no referent the polity supplies. Ma Xiulan's audit passes end to end: inclusion proofs, consistency proofs, three valid co-signatures. What it attests is self-consistency. T8's defence is arithmetic plus INDEPENDENT witnesses; remove the second factor and the arithmetic still runs, the way a notary's stamp still embosses after the notary joins the firm. The strain, precisely: the protocol cannot manufacture independence, only make its absence legible — and here it does, to any verifier who checks the witness list against the world rather than against the log.

THE KERNEL'S ONE-SENTENCE DESIGN, WITH THE SIGNS FLIPPED. The kernel's asymmetry is: mechanisms cheap to change, a person's standing as free and equal nearly impossible to change (§5). This legal order exhibits the same asymmetry inverted. What is entrenched: Article 1, amended in 2018 to read 'Leadership by the Communist Party of China is the defining feature of socialism with Chinese characteristics', with damaging the socialist system prohibited in the same breath — the Party's standing, pinned. What is cheap: the rights chapter amends by a two-thirds vote of a chamber that votes near-unanimously, and Article 51 subordinates every right in it to 'the interests of the state, society or collectives' — UDHR 29's limitation clause run backwards, licensing limitation instead of limiting it, with no 'solely' and no democratic-society test. Two constitutions, two Article 51s, is the corpus's neatest accident: the EU's Art 51 gates where its Charter applies; the PRC's Art 51 caps what any right can claim. One is a scope condition a lawyer can argue into; the other is an override no argument survives.

THE COUNTERFACTUAL THIS COLUMN RUNS ON, OWNED. Every national column is a fiction — Britain and Germany have not adopted the kernel either. But their fiction is one move: the state adopts. This column's fiction is two: the state adopts, and the state tolerates what adoption means — an append-only public record of enacted law, rights classifications and coercive acts that the operator cannot quietly rewrite. In the polity being measured, that second move is the harder one: real-name registration governs network activity, cryptography services are a licensed regime, and an unrewritable record independently witnessed is precisely the artifact the order is built not to permit. The scenario grants the fiction because the column exists to measure the instrument (coverage 19/30, remedy 'none', scope unconditional), not the odds of adoption. The consequence is measured separately, in the trajectory: where the UK's improvement path stalls at the last step — the sovereign will not bind itself — this legal order's path stalls at the first, because the transparency log is not a sovereignty-free move here. Legibility is the concession an unaccountable power cannot make (docs/cn-trajectory.md).
